title: Diablo II
edition_pinned: PC, Lord of Destruction 1.14d — pins the Classic Battle.net version Blizzard required in 2021, excluding Resurrected systems.
moat: Item affix rolls, sockets, and skill-point commitments make each drop a build-routing decision.

loop_skeleton.description: Kill a pack, inspect drops, change equipment or skill allocation, reach a waypoint, then reset the world to run it again.

depth_mechanisms.name: layered item search
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Base types, affixes, rarity classes, sockets, runes, sets, uniques, charms, and cube recipes create intersecting equipment searches.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If affix rolls and sockets are removed, repeated boss runs yield fewer equipment decisions per hour because only named item upgrades remain.

depth_mechanisms.name: skill commitment
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Each level grants a choice between unlocking a prerequisite path and strengthening a learned skill; item bonuses then change the result.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If skill points unlock every class skill at fixed levels, same-class characters converge on one action set before the second difficulty.

depth_mechanisms.name: persistent character, reset world
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Saving retains the character and waypoints but reloads monsters, treasure, and world placement, converting cleared spaces into repeatable resource routes.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If monster and treasure state persists after clearing, waypoint-to-boss routes stop producing repeat runs and the item search loses its replenishing input.

depth_mechanisms.name: build-state combat tests
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Random equipment and chosen skill paths determine whether a character can cross later difficulty and multiplayer checks.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If late encounters ignore equipment and skill allocation, clear rates cease to separate builds with different damage, resistance, and control profiles.

feel_signature: A click drops a pack; an item label stops movement; the player compares breakpoints, equips or discards, then enters the next reset map.
second_session_answer: A return starts another item sample and another skill path against regenerated routes; the player tests whether a build reaches a breakpoint sooner.
packaging_lessons: Ship one end-to-end class path with item generation and a repeatable boss route before multiplying classes, because skills and treasure rates must be tested together.
Use the same character-persistence contract across solo and multiplayer, with reset worlds and waypoints defining session boundaries.
Expose recipe inputs and socket order as data so the loot layer can add conversions without changing the kill-and-inspect loop.
anti_lessons: Do not copy the one-year crunch reported by the project lead; scope and scheduling are system constraints.
Do not make irreversible skill spending the sole source of experimentation; the pinned patch includes a drop-fed respec path.
Do not reproduce item volume without filters, comparison support, and a disposal rule; labels otherwise halt the combat loop without yielding decisions.

system_bom.note: Monster packs emit experience and item samples, feeding the next equipment or skill decision.
Base items, quality tiers, affixes, sockets, runes, sets, uniques, charms, and crafted items supply build inputs.
Class trees turn level gains into prerequisite, specialization, and skill-rank choices.
Experience grants skill choices while item requirements gate when equipment can enter the build.
Equipment slots, backpack cells, stash space, charms, and the cube force keep, equip, convert, or discard decisions.
Cube recipes and ordered rune insertion convert drops into repair, upgrade, crafted-item, and runeword outcomes.
Character and waypoint persistence survive sessions while monster and treasure state resets.
Battle.net parties combine class skills and equipment states, adding party-composition checks to the same character model.
Ground labels, item tooltips, skill trees, inventory cells, and waypoint selection expose the decisions that interrupt combat.

citations.claim: Edition pin: Blizzard support required Lord of Destruction 1.14d to connect to Classic Battle.net and supplied the PC patch link.
citations.source: Blizzard Legacy Games Tech Support, 'Diablo II (2000) Unable to identify version [2021]': https://us.forums.blizzard.com/en/blizzard/t/diablo-ii-2000-unable-to-identify-version-2021/17508

citations.claim: Moat: item qualities, affixes, sockets, runes, sets, uniques, charms, crafted items, and cube recipes layer multiple decisions onto a drop.
citations.source: Blizzard, The Arreat Summit, Items index: https://classic.battle.net/diablo2exp/items/

citations.claim: Depth — layered item search: rune order creates runewords, charms consume backpack space, and cube recipes transform item inputs.
citations.source: Blizzard, The Arreat Summit, Items index: https://classic.battle.net/diablo2exp/items/ ; Horadric Cube: https://classic.battle.net/diablo2exp/items/cube.shtml

citations.claim: Depth — skill commitment: level gains can strengthen a skill or pursue prerequisites, producing specialization and divergent same-class builds.
citations.source: Erich Schaefer, “Postmortem: Blizzard's Diablo II,” Game Developer Magazine, October 2000: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/postmortem-blizzard-s-i-diablo-ii-i-

citations.claim: Depth — persistent character, reset world: reload returns the character to town, resets monsters and treasure, and preserves waypoint access.
citations.source: Erich Schaefer, “Postmortem: Blizzard's Diablo II,” Game Developer Magazine, October 2000, save-game discussion: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/postmortem-blizzard-s-i-diablo-ii-i-

citations.claim: Depth — build-state combat tests: the team had to replay the game because equipment, skill paths, multiplayer, and difficulty modes changed encounter balance.
citations.source: Erich Schaefer, “Postmortem: Blizzard's Diablo II,” Game Developer Magazine, October 2000, quality-assurance discussion: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/postmortem-blizzard-s-i-diablo-ii-i-

citations.claim: Feel signature: real-time monster clearing alternates with item inspection and character-build decisions.
citations.source: Erich Schaefer, “Postmortem: Blizzard's Diablo II,” Game Developer Magazine, October 2000; Blizzard, The Arreat Summit, Basic Item Information: https://classic.battle.net/diablo2exp/items/basics.shtml

citations.claim: Second-session answer: regenerated monsters and treasure let a character refight routes for experience and loot, while skill branches support another build path.
citations.source: Erich Schaefer, “Postmortem: Blizzard's Diablo II,” Game Developer Magazine, October 2000, skill-tree and save-game discussions: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/postmortem-blizzard-s-i-diablo-ii-i-

citations.claim: Packaging lesson — first class slice: item rates and skill paths could not be balanced apart from a full playthrough; this supports shipping one integrated path before class multiplication.
citations.source: Erich Schaefer, “Postmortem: Blizzard's Diablo II,” Game Developer Magazine, October 2000, quality-assurance discussion: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/postmortem-blizzard-s-i-diablo-ii-i-

citations.claim: Packaging lesson — persistence contract: the team made solo and multiplayer use one reset-world save model and added waypoints for return access.
citations.source: Erich Schaefer, “Postmortem: Blizzard's Diablo II,” Game Developer Magazine, October 2000, save-game discussion: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/postmortem-blizzard-s-i-diablo-ii-i-

citations.claim: Packaging lesson — data-driven conversions: documented cube equations and ordered rune sets add item outcomes through recipes.
citations.source: Blizzard, The Arreat Summit, Horadric Cube: https://classic.battle.net/diablo2exp/items/cube.shtml ; Items index: https://classic.battle.net/diablo2exp/items/

citations.claim: BOM — play-loop, save-state: reset monsters and treasure replenish experience and loot while town and waypoints bound sessions.
citations.source: Erich Schaefer, “Postmortem: Blizzard's Diablo II,” Game Developer Magazine, October 2000, save-game discussion: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/postmortem-blizzard-s-i-diablo-ii-i-

citations.claim: BOM — loot and inventory: Blizzard documents item tiers, equipment, backpack and stash storage, charms, sockets, and ground-item persistence.
citations.source: Blizzard, The Arreat Summit, Items index: https://classic.battle.net/diablo2exp/items/ ; Basic Item Information: https://classic.battle.net/diablo2exp/items/basics.shtml

citations.claim: BOM — skills and leveling: each experience level supports choosing or strengthening class-tree skills; item bonuses can raise learned skill ranks.
citations.source: Erich Schaefer, “Postmortem: Blizzard's Diablo II,” Game Developer Magazine, October 2000; Blizzard, Basic Skill Information: https://classic.battle.net/diablo2exp/skills/basics.shtml

citations.claim: BOM — crafting: cube recipes upgrade runes, repair gear, remove socket contents, and produce crafted items; rune order produces runewords.
citations.source: Blizzard, The Arreat Summit, Horadric Cube: https://classic.battle.net/diablo2exp/items/cube.shtml ; Items index: https://classic.battle.net/diablo2exp/items/

citations.claim: BOM — multiplayer: Battle.net support and multiplayer difficulty required testing parties whose class skills interact.
citations.source: Erich Schaefer, “Postmortem: Blizzard's Diablo II,” Game Developer Magazine, October 2000, Battle.net and quality-assurance discussions: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/postmortem-blizzard-s-i-diablo-ii-i-

citations.claim: BOM — UI: item descriptions, backpack cells, cube interaction, skill-tree allocation, and waypoint travel surface loop decisions.
citations.source: Blizzard, Diablo II manual: https://download.blizzard.com/pub/misc/Diablo%20II%20Manual.pdf ; Blizzard, The Arreat Summit, Horadric Cube: https://classic.battle.net/diablo2exp/items/cube.shtml

citations.claim: Anti-lesson — production: the project lead reports a yearlong crunch and relationship costs.
citations.source: Erich Schaefer, “Postmortem: Blizzard's Diablo II,” Game Developer Magazine, October 2000, conclusion: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/postmortem-blizzard-s-i-diablo-ii-i-

citations.claim: Anti-lesson — respec support: patch-era skill documentation lists the Token of Absolution recipe from Hell act-boss essences.
citations.source: Blizzard, The Arreat Summit, Basic Skill Information: https://classic.battle.net/diablo2exp/skills/basics.shtml

citations.claim: Anti-lesson — item volume inference: Blizzard documents ground labels, inventory limits, item comparison fields, and sell or discard paths; the factory must package those controls with drop volume.
citations.source: Blizzard, Diablo II manual, inventory and item sections: https://download.blizzard.com/pub/misc/Diablo%20II%20Manual.pdf
